narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 24, Year 1 decile rank of 5th (slightly below the average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 3rd (well below the typical 5.53), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 42.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their program. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward you passing by standing out above the average of 3.21 and signaling solid academic preparation. However, your LSAT score was the next most influential factor and worked against you, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure since your score of 24 is notably lower than the typical 36.70. Your Year 1 decile rank also played a key role, though less powerfully, by nudging things toward passing as it was reasonably close to average performance in your school. Overall, while your GPA and early grades offer some reassurance compared to peers, the low LSAT score dominated the model's concern about bar exam success, tipping the balance despite your full-time commitment and middle-income background aligning with most applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many from similar backgrounds thrive with stronger test prep—but the model saw your LSAT as a red flag relative to what predicts bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your later-year grades or gaining relevant experience might also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore retake strategies or alternative programs that value your GPA strengths. With targeted effort, you can reshape this outcome and pursue your legal goals.
